note: Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1542 is valid and official (EUR-Lex). Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 exists and Articles 7 & 8 govern carbon footprint declarations. However, the mandatory_status.target and gap are materially incomplete/misleading: delegated acts for EV batteries were adopted April 2024 (methodology + declaration format); the EV battery CFD applies from 18 February 2025; industrial batteries (≥2 kWh, which includes BESS) CFD applies from 18 February 2026. The fragment says 'phase-in dates not yet finalised [UNVERIFIED]' which is no longer accurate for EV or industrial categories — dates are now known. BESS-specific (industrial ≥2 kWh) mandatory date is 18 February 2026. Fragment overstates uncertainty; needs update to reflect adopted delegated acts.

Source URL valid and official. Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 Articles 18–24 and Annexes VIII–IX on CE marking and conformity assessment are correctly cited. mandatory_status.target is accurate — CE marking is mandatory for all batteries placed on EU market covered by the Regulation. Gap analysis (CCC vs EU conformity assessment) is accurate. No material errors found.

Source URL valid and official. Article 48 due diligence exists and is correctly described. However, the mandatory_status.target date '18 August 2025' is now outdated and incorrect: Regulation (EU) 2025/1561 (published 30 July 2025) amended Regulation 2023/1542 and postponed Article 48 due diligence obligations from 18 August 2025 to 18 August 2027. The fragment's '[UNVERIFIED — confirm exact application date]' caveat does not capture this critical amendment. Date must be updated to 18 August 2027.

Source URL valid and official. Articles 13–14 (labelling) and Article 77 (Battery Passport) exist in the Regulation. However, mandatory_status.target dates need correction: (1) Labelling — Article 13 general labelling applies from 18 August 2025 (separate collection symbol), with further labelling requirements from 18 August 2026 or 18 months after implementing act, whichever is later. The fragment states '18 August 2025 [UNVERIFIED]' which is correct for basic labelling but incomplete for full labelling. (2) Battery Passport — date is 18 February 2027 (confirmed across multiple sources), NOT a generic '2027 [UNVERIFIED]'. The specific date should be stated as 18 February 2027. Delegated acts for Battery Passport data attributes remain pending as of June 2026. Overall the dates require precision updates.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official (LVD 2014/35/EU on EUR-Lex). EN IEC 62619:2022 exists (IEC webstore publication/64073, Edition 2.0, May 2022) and has been adopted by CENELEC as EN IEC 62619:2022 (approved by CENELEC 2022-06-28, supersedes EN 62619:2017). It is listed as a harmonised standard under LVD via Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2023/2723. EN IEC 63056:2020 also exists. mandatory_status.target correctly states LVD CE marking is mandatory. Gap analysis is accurate.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1542 is valid. IEC 62933-5-2:2020 exists (IEC webstore publication/32177) and was adopted as EN IEC 62933-5-2:2020 by CENELEC. However, IEC 62933-5-2:2020 has been superseded by IEC 62933-5-2:2025 (Edition 2.0, published December 2025; IEC webstore publication/68297). The fragment cites only the 2020 edition. Additionally, as of June 2026 the EN IEC 62933-5-2 harmonised standard listing in the EU OJ is under review due to the Malamud ruling affecting ISO/IEC-derived harmonised standards — harmonised status should be flagged as uncertain. The fragment's reference to IEC 62933-5-2:2020 as the current standard is outdated; the 2025 edition now supersedes it.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official (LVD 2014/35/EU). LVD 2014/35/EU exists and applies to equipment in the stated voltage ranges. EN IEC 62619, EN IEC 63056, and IEC 62933-5-2 are correctly identified as key harmonised standards. mandatory_status.target correctly states CE marking under LVD is mandatory. Gap analysis (no bilateral MRA, need for EU-recognised lab tests, EU DoC requirement) is accurate and well-supported.

Source URL https://webstore.iec.ch/publication/61289 appears to be a stale/incorrect IEC webstore URL — the verified IEC 62933-5-2:2020 page is at https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/32177 (confirmed). The URL in the fragment may be dead or redirect incorrectly; the /publication/ path without /en/ is an older URL format that may not resolve. Recommend replacing source.url with https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/32177 (for the 2020 edition) or https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/68297 (for the current 2025 edition). Additionally, IEC 62933-5-2:2020 has been superseded by IEC 62933-5-2:2025 (December 2025); the fragment should reference the current edition. The mandatory_status.target statement about LVD/MD harmonisation status is correctly flagged [UNVERIFIED] — as of June 2026 the EN IEC harmonised standard OJ citation status is under review due to the Malamud ruling. UL 9540A is correctly characterised as not mandated by EU law.

Source URL https://unece.org/transport/standards/transport/dangerous-goods/un-manual-tests-and-criteria-rev7-and-amend1 is outdated: Rev.7 has been superseded by Rev.8 (2023) with Amendment 1 (2025). The current edition is Rev.8; the official page is https://unece.org/transport/standards/transport/dangerous-goods/un-manual-tests-and-criteria-rev8-2023. The fragment also cites 'ST/SG/AC.10/11/Rev.7 and Amend.1' as the applicable document reference — this should be updated to Rev.8. The ADR 2025 citation is accurate (confirmed UNECE official ADR 2025 pages exist at https://unece.org/adr-2025-files). mandatory_status.target is correctly described as mandatory for all international transport. Recommend replacing source.url with the Rev.8 UNECE page.

Source URL https://unece.org/transport/dangerous-goods/adr-2025 resolves to the official UNECE ADR 2025 page (confirmed: https://unece.org/adr-2025-files and related pages exist). ADR 2025, IMDG Code Amendment 41-22, and IATA DGR 65th Edition 2024 citations are accurate. Packing instructions P903/P908 and special provisions cited are consistent with ADR 2025 content. mandatory_status.target correctly states these are legally binding. Gap analysis is accurate and well-reasoned. No material errors found.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32016R0631 is valid and official (Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/631 RfG on EUR-Lex). EN 50549-1:2019 and EN 50549-2:2019 exist as CENELEC standards for generating plants connected to LV/MV distribution networks (confirmed via search). Regulation (EU) 2016/631 RfG is correctly described as binding EU law. VDE-AR-N 4105/4110 and G99 are real national grid codes. mandatory_status.target is accurately described. Gap analysis (GB/T 34120 not equivalent, re-testing required, different communication protocols) is accurate.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1542 is valid and official. LVD 2014/35/EU, EMC Directive 2014/30/EU, and Battery Regulation 2023/1542 are correctly cited as the applicable EU framework for BESS CE marking. EN IEC 62619:2022 and EN 61000-6-2/6-4 are valid harmonised standards. mandatory_status.target is accurate. The [UNVERIFIED] caveat on implementing acts for Reg 2023/1542 assessment modules remains appropriate as of June 2026.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official. LVD Articles 7/Annex III/IV for DoC and technical file are correctly cited. Battery Regulation Articles 17-18 and 77-78 exist. The Battery Passport date is correctly flagged as subject to delegated acts; the confirmed date (18 February 2027) should be noted but the [UNVERIFIED] caveat on technical specifications remains appropriate. mandatory_status.target is accurate. Gap analysis is sound.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32019R1020 is valid and official (Market Surveillance Regulation). Regulation 2019/1020 Art. 4-5 (authorised representative), Battery Regulation 2023/1542 Art. 56-61 (producer responsibility), and WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU Art. 16-17 are correctly cited. mandatory_status.target accurately describes the mandatory EU authorised representative and producer registration obligations. The [UNVERIFIED] caveat on Reg 2023/1542 producer scope for stationary BESS vs. industrial batteries remains appropriate given pending implementing acts.
